key_insight: A Bayesian finite element model reconstructs vascular velocity and pressure fields from sparse noisy measurements while explicitly quantifying uncertainty.
engineering_relevance: This enables more reliable hemodynamic inference for patient-specific vessels when inlet and outlet boundary conditions are unknown, supporting clinical quantities like pressure drop, flow rate, and wall shear stress.
method: Represent velocity and pressure with Taylor-Hood finite element basis functions.
Use physics-informed maximum-entropy priors plus a noise-model likelihood to form a posterior.
Compute MAP estimates with sparse nonlinear least squares, then apply a Laplace approximation for uncertainty propagation.
evidence_signal: Evaluated on patient-specific cerebral aneurysm, aortic aneurysm, and aortic coarctation geometries, where it outperformed tricubic interpolation and matched a PINN for velocity/pressure reconstruction while improving wall shear stress estimates.
watch_item: The approach depends on an accurate finite-element mesh, a valid noise model, and a Laplace approximation, so uncertainty estimates may be less reliable if these assumptions are violated.
paper_title: Bayesian finite element regression for vascular flow reconstruction with quantified uncertainty
